note: A red sandstone tenement with a coherent row of shopfronts below (signs reading '...Of Leith Walk' and 'Adams Whyte Cri...' cut only by the frame edge, not the geometry), consistent windows and no stretching visible.
Same red-brick tenement block with a legible 'Shug McGubbin' NPC dialogue over it; the facade behind reads as a real, intact building with no distortion or repetition.
Camera sits almost inside the wall, so the brick/window texture is blown up into a blurry, low-resolution smear that no longer reads as any real material or shopfront.
The cream building's window bays look subtly skewed/warped relative to one another, as if a photo taken at an angle was pasted flat onto the wall rather than aligned to the geometry.
The ground-floor shop signage is sliced mid-word ('ED...' / '...RER...') by a plain dark filler panel, and the same weathered facade texture repeats identically on both sides of that panel.
Wider view confirms the identical grimy tenement texture is mirrored on both sides of a blank dark divider wall, with shop text still cropped mid-word beneath it.
A coherent fire-damaged/burnt tenement facade with consistent window bays; the ground-floor sign is largely obscured by the NPC dialogue box but the building itself reads as real and derelict.

At a building corner, the same 'Desi Pakwan' shopfront (including the window bays above it) is duplicated on both visible faces, reading as a mirrored copy rather than distinct sides of a real building.
The view down this stretch shows mostly flat, blank, untextured wall faces with no shopfront or architectural detail at all, breaking the illusion well before roof/pavement quality matters.

A cream sandstone building with red-trimmed windows and a ground-floor arcade reads convincingly, with legible neighbouring signage ('VAS MOTORS', '...D WORKS') consistent with a real derelict high street.
Camera is jammed extremely close to an illuminated sign, resolving only as huge blurry glowing letters ('M' and fragments) against a dark void, with no coherent shopfront or building visible.

Readable ground-floor signage ('...RESSES', 'Leith Makers') sits under a derelict stone tenement, but a distinct blank white vertical seam cuts between the pale stone building and the adjacent red-brick building, cropping the frontage awkwardly rather than at a natural building edge.
Camera is pressed right up against a window/door frame, producing an out-of-focus close-up of wood trim and glass panes with no legible shopfront, signage, or building identity.
A row of distinct, well-proportioned tenement buildings (partly obscured by a parked van) with straight verticals, plausible partial shop signage, and no visible stretching or repeating tiling.
Extreme close-up on a dark red door/pillar frame, heavily blurred, with no readable signage or sense of what shop this is.

A cream Georgian stone facade with well-formed, correctly proportioned windows and cornicing above a van and crate that block the ground floor; no stretching, wrong perspective, or repeated tiling visible in the parts shown.
A flat, completely blank untextured dark slab sits in the middle of the building row, visibly slicing through the ground-floor signage rather than aligning with a natural shop or building boundary.

A convincing sooty, fire-damaged tenement facade with broken windows and scorch staining reads as a coherent derelict building; a thin bright vertical sliver at the extreme right frame edge is a minor, easy-to-miss artifact.
The view shows plain, blank brick side-walls of an alley leading to a flat, featureless grey block with no windows or signage — it does not read as a real building frontage at all.
Distant view shows a flat blank grey box and an isolated, seemingly disconnected thin building sliver with windows floating apart from any continuous street row, breaking the sense of a real street.
Extreme close-up shows only blurred fragments of two shutter signs with no coherent shopfront visible in frame.
Readable, distinct shopfronts (FRITES, Arkay Imaging, Volunteer Edinburgh, Ladbrokes) on varied period façades read convincingly as a real derelict high street.
The centre building is a flat, featureless dark box with no windows or shopfront detail, sitting incongruously between two normal buildings.
Same flat textureless dark box building dominates the frame, and the building to its right has a blank white ground-floor front with no signage or shop identity.
Close-up of a burnt-out tenement above a legible pub sign, "The Fairy Boy Of Leith", reads as a plausible derelict building.
The upper floors show the same window-bay shape and scorch/damage pattern repeated identically across all five bays, reading as tiled wallpaper rather than a real façade.
Extreme close zoom on a shutter shows only the word "Breezo" against near-total darkness, with no shopfront context visible.
Two adjacent building modules share an identical upper-storey window and cornice texture duplicated side by side with a visible seam.
Above the legible "Mo's Sharp Styl..." barber sign, the corner turret shows a warped, angled paper-like texture that doesn't match the flat vertical stonework around it.
Despite legible ground-floor shops (AMOR, Kukina, Taste of Poland), the central building's five upper-storey window bays repeat the identical tile and grime pattern like wallpaper.
Camera is jammed almost inside a shutter/signage at point-blank range, rendering as a blurry mipmap smear with only fragments of two shop signs faintly legible.
A row of tenement fronts with legible shopfront signage (FRITES, Arkay Imaging, Volunteer Edinburgh, Ladbrokes) reads as a coherent, if grimy, derelict high street.
A weathered tenement with a clear 'CAKE BOX' shutter sign sits beside a plain dark gable wall, reading as a believable derelict street corner.
Same corner from further back, showing a blank dark side-elevation flanked by a sandstone tenement with a bright ground-floor unit, coherent as a building's side wall.
Camera is again pressed right up against a surface, producing an out-of-focus blur of a corrugated shutter and a partial window/sign shape that cannot be read as any specific shopfront.
Close-in view of a sandstone tenement with fire-damaged upper windows and a legible 'The Fairy Boy Of Leith' shopfront sign underneath, reads as a coherent derelict pub frontage.
A tenement row with legible 'BEST KEBAB HOUSE' and 'The Fairy Boy Of Leith' signage, a parked car, and a lamp-post/NPC in the foreground reads as a plausible derelict street.
A row of shopfronts (AMOR, alterations/dry cleaners, Kukina, Taste of Poland) beneath a narrow infill building and a wider sandstone tenement reads as coherent, legible street frontage.
Same AMOR/Kukina/Taste of Poland row viewed from this camera spot, legible shopfronts and proportionate windows on the narrow red infill building between the two tenements.

A narrow alley view with a plain dark side-wall on the left and a curved corner sandstone building on the right bearing a legible 'Mo's Sharp Styl[e]' barbershop sign, reads as a believable derelict lane.
Close-up of a shuttered ground-floor unit reading 'STARLIGHT' beside a 'Scotm[id]' frontage, with plain sash windows above — coherent, unstretched, no wrong-perspective cues.
Whole tenement block reads clearly as Starlight and Scotmid shopfronts under regular symmetric sash windows, cropped at a natural building edge, no repeating or stretched textures visible.
A furniture-shop window (partial signage reading '...nardo's furniture') with visible furniture silhouettes inside reads as a coherent, specific shop.

The recessed courtyard revealing Fransos/Barnardo's furniture is flanked by two narrow brick returns whose window-bay pattern is an obvious mirrored duplicate of each other, reading as the same tile cloned rather than distinct architecture.
Camera is pressed flush against a plain, featureless roller shutter filling the whole frame with no signage or identifying detail.
A distant, dimly-lit plain wall across the street with an NPC in the foreground — too dark to make out detail, but nothing legible is wrong either.
Oblique grazing-angle view down a terrace showing a legible '...o's Sharp Style Bar' barbershop frontage with window verticals converging plausibly with the camera angle.
Row of buildings including 'Sharp Style Bar' and a 'Word of ... Old...' shop reads as coherent adjoining frontages; no stretching or repeated tiling in the facades themselves.
Close, dark crop on an illuminated sign reading 'Church Leith' (part of a longer name) — legible and coherent, though the shot is tight and low-lit.
Wider view clearly reads 'Grace Church Leith' as a specific, coherent building frontage; the plain brick wall above the sign is unadorned rather than showing any repeated window pattern.
Camera is jammed almost inside the wall so only a heavily blurred fragment of a sign is visible against a featureless black void.
Clear tenement row with legible shopfronts (AIRLINK TRAVEL, BODEGA LEITH, OTHERWORLD) and plausible weathered sandstone upper floors, reads convincingly as a real derelict street.
Extreme close-up blur shows only a partial sign fragment against a shutter, too close and out of focus to read as any specific shop.
Legible signage on a weathered tenement corner with a plant growing from the roofline, reads as a believable derelict building.
Camera is essentially clipped into the facade producing a dark, heavily blurred smear of repeating rectangular shapes with no legible shopfront or detail.
Clean, legible shopfronts (JUST COFFEE, TESCO EXPRESS) beneath a plain plank-clad upper wall; no stretching, cropping, or repetition issues visible.
Very close and blurred, showing only a partial sign fragment on a shutter against a plain plank wall, not legible as a specific shop.
Shopfronts HADDINGTON... and INDI... read clearly (the latter cropped naturally at the screen edge, not mid-facade), building above is a plain flat wall.
Camera is pressed up against a pole/wall with heavy blur, showing only an isolated letter fragment with no readable shopfront.
Good corner view of a cracked, weathered sandstone tenement with legible shopfronts (OH DEER, TWELVE TRIANGLE...) and varied non-repeating upper-floor windows.
A row of legible, distinct shopfronts sits below two adjoining tenement facades, one burnt/derelict and one intact cream sandstone, both reading as real buildings.
Wider view of the same corner shows a fire-damaged sandstone tenement next to a cleaner cream building, with varied broken windows and curtains.
Camera is pressed right up against a shop sign showing only the blurry, oversized letters "END", which does not read as any coherent shopfront.
A glaring blank white vertical strip/seam runs down the boundary between two buildings where a texture appears to be missing or blown out, breaking the illusion immediately.
A distant tenement corner with a partly visible shopfront and a shadowed blank gable end wall reads as a plausible side-street view.
The building's brick/window texture ends abruptly at a rounded corner into a blank grey curved wall, with the same bright white seam artifact visible as in the west-far pose.
Extreme close-up blur on a sign showing only fragments of letters, unreadable and not identifiable as any specific shop.
A damaged sandstone tenement row with legible signage (Phone Spot, Digital Image, partially cropped Hair and Jajira at frame edges) reads convincingly as a derelict street.
A narrow reddish infill building between two wider tenements, with clear shop names, reads as a believable mixed streetscape.
A rounded corner building displays a garbled sign that does not resolve into any coherent shop name, even though the neighbouring sign is legible.

The central sandstone tenement's facade is mirrored/duplicated across a vertical seam, so the shopfront text, flags, and window bays appear twice as a symmetric reflection rather than a continuous real frontage.

Same mirrored-facade artifact is visible from further back — the stone building's windows and ground-floor signage repeat symmetrically around a centre line instead of reading as one coherent building.
The frame is dominated by a flat, untextured dark gable/side wall with no shopfront or window detail at all, save a sliver of a textured facade cut off at the left edge.
Again mostly blank dark side walls stretching into the distance, with only a fragment of a shop sign legible at the left edge — no coherent frontage to read.
The central handsome arched-doorway sandstone building reads well, but the buildings flanking it on both left and right edges show the same dark window-bay texture tiled repeatedly like wallpaper.

The central beige building's windows and stonework are visibly slanted/skewed rather than vertical, and the adjoining Ladbrokes unit is cropped awkwardly mid-facade, indicating an angled source photo pasted flat onto the geometry.
